publications.title: How an alloreactive T-cell receptor achieves peptide and MHC specificity.
publications.journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America
publications.authors: Wang Y, Singh NK, Spear TT, Hellman LM, Piepenbrink KH, McMahan RH, Rosen HR, Vander Kooi CW, Nishimura MI, Baker BM.
publications.executive_summary: The HCV1406 TCR, isolated from an HLA-A2+ liver allograft recipient with hepatitis C virus infection, recognizes the HCV nonstructural protein 3 (NS3) epitope 1406–1415 presented by HLA-A2 with high specificity despite its alloreactive origin. Structural analysis reveals that this specificity arises through cooperativity between structural mimicry effects and a critical peptide "hot spot" residue, both of which depend on features unique to both the allogeneic MHC and the NS3 epitope. This work resolves the apparent paradox that alloreactive TCRs can achieve high peptide and MHC specificity, challenging the assumption that allorecognition is inherently degenerate or nonspecific. The findings have direct implications for harnessing alloreactive TCRs as immunotherapeutics—particularly for viral infections in transplant settings—where their ability to bypass central tolerance while maintaining epitope specificity could limit graft-versus-host disease.
